title: Claude Code for GTM — 3 Workflows
body:
Claude Code hype is real. 

Here are 3 GTM flows we launched this year at our $7M ARR company: 

Why is Claude code trending right now? 

Not because it writes code for you.

Because it lets non-technical GTM teams build autonomous workflows that used to require a full engineering sprint.

Here some examples: 

1.End-to-End Outbound Campaign Builder

- Upload a CSV of companies

- Claude Code tiers them into Tier 1/2/3 using your ICP scoring criteria (pure logic — no AI hallucinations)

- Finds the right contacts via Apollo's API

- Enriches missing emails through secondary providers

- Generates personalized sequences from your saved copy frameworks

- Pushes the full campaign live into Instantly

2. LinkedIn Engagement → Outbound Pipeline

- Scrapes everyone who likes or comments on your LinkedIn posts

- Runs ICP scoring + CRM de-duplication automatically

- Finds their work email via enrichment providers

- Routes qualified leads into an outbound sequence

- Runs weekly on autopilot, no one presses a button

Bonus: extend it to monitor competitor posts and keyword-based content. 
Anyone posting about your category enters your pipeline. 
One team replaced $300+/month in scraping tools with this single agent.

3. Ad Campaign Builder Across LinkedIn, Meta & Google

- Fill in a Google Sheet: campaign name, targeting, ad copy, landing page

- Run one prompt in Claude Code

- It calls the LinkedIn/Meta/Google Ads API automatically

- Pulls the right creatives from Google Drive

- Launches the campaign across platforms

A full campaign that used to take 30+ minutes in the UI now takes under 2 minutes without touching a single dashboard.

The real unlock isn't any single workflow.

It's that your scoring criteria, copy frameworks, and past campaign data all live in one place 

and compound every time you run a new campaign.

Which of these three would have the biggest impact on your GTM motion right now?

title: LinkedIn Ad Campaign Builder
body:
Building a full LinkedIn ad campaign used to take me 30+ minutes of fighting the UI.

Now it takes under 2 minutes. I don't touch LinkedIn's campaign manager at all.

Here's what I built with Claude Code:

A Google Sheet where I fill in:
• Campaign name
• Targeting criteria (ABM list, job titles, bottom of funnel, etc.)
• Ad copy reference
• Landing page + CTA

Then I run one prompt in Claude Code. It reads the sheet, calls the LinkedIn Ads API, pulls the right ad creative from Google Drive, and creates the campaign live.

I built the same thing for Meta Ads and Google Ads.

No MCP server exists for LinkedIn Ads, so I fed Claude Code the API docs, let it figure out the right calls, and saved the working scripts as reusable "skills."

We now manage hundreds of thousands in ad spend this way across all three platforms.

The craziest part? I'm not a developer. I did this in plan mode, one prompt at a time.

If you're running paid ads at scale and still manually building campaigns in the UI — what would you automate first if you had this?

title: Full Outbound Campaign in 20 Minutes
body:
153 contacts. Enriched emails. Personalized copy. Live campaign in Instantly.

All from a CSV — without opening Clay, writing a formula, or copy-pasting anything.

Here's the exact flow I built in Claude Code:

Step 1 — Score your list
Upload companies. Claude Code reads your ICP scoring criteria and tiers every company into Tier 1/2/3 using Python. Zero AI hallucination risk — pure logic on raw data.

Step 2 — Find contacts
"Find me sales leaders at Tier 1 and Tier 2 companies." Claude Code calls the Apollo API. 153 contacts in ~90 seconds.

Step 3 — Enrich emails
Missing emails get filled via secondary enrichment providers. 149 of 153 came back verified.

Step 4 — Write copy
Claude Code reads your saved copy frameworks and generates personalized sequences with conditional logic by persona/industry.

Step 5 — Push to Instantly
Campaign appears live — fully loaded with leads and sequences.

The biggest unlock isn't speed. It's that your scoring criteria, copy frameworks, and campaign history compound over time in one place.

What part of your outbound workflow still requires the most manual effort?

title: Boris Cherny's 8 Claude Code Tips
body:
The creator of Claude Code just shared how his own team uses it.

Here are the 8 tips that actually matter:

1. Run tasks in parallel
Spin up 3-5 git worktrees at once, each running its own Claude session. It's the single biggest productivity unlock — hop between them in one keystroke.

2. Always start complex tasks in plan mode
Pour your energy into the plan so Claude can 1-shot the implementation. One person writes the plan, a second Claude reviews it as a staff engineer.

3. Invest in your CLAUDE.md
After every correction, end with: "Update your CLAUDE.md so you don't make that mistake again." Keep iterating until Claude's mistake rate measurably drops.

4. Build your own skills
If you do something more than once a day, turn it into a reusable skill or command. Commit them to git and reuse across every project.

5. Let Claude fix its own bugs
Enable the Slack MCP, paste a thread, and say "fix." Point it at Docker logs. Just say "Go for the failing CI tests." Don't micromanage — it's surprisingly capable.

6. Prompt like you mean it
• Say "Grill me on these changes and don't make a PR until I pass your test"
• After a mediocre fix: "Knowing everything you know now, scrap this and implement the elegant solution"
• Write detailed specs. The more specific, the better the output.

7. Use subagents
Offload individual tasks to subagents to keep your main context window clean. For complex problems, run 5+ at once in parallel.

8. Use it for data & analytics
Ask Claude to use the bq CLI to pull and analyze metrics on the fly. The team hasn't written a line of SQL in 6+ months.

There's no one right way to use Claude Code.

But if the team that built it is doing all of the above — that's a pretty good place to start.

Which of these are you not doing yet?
designInstructions:
Caroussel style. 

1st page: HEADLINE (top, centered): 'The creator of Claude Code shared 8 tips his team actually uses.' Bold, large.

each tip card (one line each):
01 Parallel Work — Spin up 3-5 worktrees at once. Biggest productivity unlock.
02 Plan Mode — Pour energy into the plan. Claude 1-shots the execution.
03 CLAUDE.md — After every mistake, update it. Mistake rate drops over time.
04  Build Skills — If you do it twice, make it a skill. Commit and reuse.
05 Self-Fix Bugs— Paste the Slack thread. Say 'fix.' Don't micromanage.
06 Better Prompts  — Write detailed specs. Challenge Claude to earn the PR.
07 Subagents — Offload tasks. Run 5+ in parallel for complex problems.
08 Data & Analytics— Use bq CLI. Zero SQL written in 6+ months.

FOOTER: 'Source: Boris Cherny, Creator of Claude Code.' 
LOGO: bottom-right corner.

title: Claude Code as a GTM Operating System
body:
Most people use AI like a smarter Google search.

The teams winning right now are using it as an operating system for their entire GTM motion.

Inside Claude Code, you maintain a folder that acts as the brain of your operation:

• CLAUDE.md — instructions and context for every agent
• Scoring criteria — how you tier your ICP
• Copy frameworks — email structures that are already converting
• API docs — so Claude never has to re-learn your tools
• Skills — reusable scripts saved every time a workflow succeeds

Before this, my copy frameworks were in Google Docs. My scoring was in Clay. My leads were in Apollo. My campaigns were in Instantly. Nothing talked to each other.

Now one prompt orchestrates the entire process.

I'm managing 10+ client campaigns simultaneously this way.

The teams that build this infrastructure early will have a compounding advantage that's very hard to catch up with.

Are you building your GTM context to compound — or starting from scratch every campaign?

title: Claude Code vs Claude Cowork — What's the Difference?
body:
Anthropic has two of the hottest agentic products right now.
                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
Most people don't know the difference or that both exist.

 Here's the breakdown:

Claude Code -> The Agent for Builders

What it is: A terminal-based agent that lives on your machine and operates like a technical co-founder.

  What it can do:
  - Reads and writes files directly on your system — no uploads
  - Runs commands, tests, and creates pull requests
  - Builds reusable skills and automated workflows
  - Runs Agent Teams: multiple agents that communicate with each other, share discoveries, and coordinate tasks in parallel
  - Stores all memory and context locally — you own everything
  - Deploys autonomous agents via Railway and N8N that run 24/7

  What it can't do:
  - Run without a terminal — there is no GUI
  - Generate formatted Excel or PowerPoint natively
  - Schedule recurring tasks without extra setup

  Who it's for: Developers, GTM operators, growth teams. Any non-technical person willing to spend a day learning the basics. The ceiling is very high.

Claude Cowork -> The Agent for Knowledge Workers

What it is: A desktop app that brings agentic capabilities to business teams — no terminal required.

What it can do:
  - Accesses local files directly without manual uploads
  - Breaks complex tasks into sub-tasks and runs them in parallel inside an isolated VM
  - Generates formatted Excel spreadsheets, PowerPoint presentations, and polished documents
  - Schedules and runs recurring tasks automatically
  - Connects natively to Google Drive, Gmail, Calendar, DocuSign, Apollo, Clay, Outreach, and 10+ tools via MCP
  - Runs as an add-in directly inside Microsoft Excel and PowerPoint

  What it can't do:
  - Build persistent agents or reusable skills like Claude Code
  - Integrate with developer workflows or run in a terminal
  - Store conversation history in audit logs — it's local only

  Who it's for: Business, ops, and knowledge workers who need autonomous task execution without touching a terminal.

Same AI. Completely different use cases.

Claude Code builds the system.
Claude Cowork runs the work.

If you're in GTM, sales, or growth, Claude Code gives you the higher ceiling.
If you're in ops, finance, or management, Cowork gets you there faster.

Which one fits your day-to-day?
source: Web Research — Claude Help Center, VentureBeat, Anthropic Blog

CAN'T DO:

CAN'T DO:

title: Running Autonomous GTM Agents 24/7
body:
1 claude code agent replaced $300/month tool
It runs our full linkedin engagement pipeline

Our outbound agents don't sleep.

Every week,  Claude Code:

→ Scrapes engagement from our team's LinkedIn posts 
→ Enriches the leads
→ routes them into outbound sequences.

Here's how we made it autonomous:

The agent = just a folder. A script, a prompt, a requirements file.
Claude Code isn't magic, it's organized code you don't have to write yourself.

Railway = where we deploy it. Once live, it runs on its own schedule, 24/7.

N8N = for complex multi-step orchestration alongside Railway.

The result for our social engager:
→ Monitors all team LinkedIn posts weekly
→ Scrapes every new engager
→ Enriches and qualifies against ICP
→ Routes into Instantly or flags in Slack for SDRs

We also monitor keyword-based content
Anyone engaging with "go-to-market" or "ABM" gets pulled into our targeting pool.

Once a workflow succeeds, you save it as a skill
↳ Reusable script Claude Code can call anytime.

Most people think Claude Code is for one-off tasks. 
The real unlock is deploying it as infrastructure that runs your GTM motion while you sleep.

What would you automate first if your agents could run 24/7?

title: AI Workforce vs AI Tools — The Shift Happening Now
body:
89% of corporate leaders say AI will reshape jobs in 2026

Workers with AI skills command salaries 56% higher than peers in identical roles without those skills.

The market is shifting from single-task AI to full AI workforces.

This is not about chatbots or copilots anymore. Companies like Canva, Autodesk, and KPMG are deploying teams of AI agents that work autonomously across entire operations.

The difference between hiring one AI tool and building an AI workforce:

→ One AI tool automates a single task

→ An AI workforce automates entire workflows

RevOps teams are building AI agents instead of hiring for three reasons.

1️⃣ 92% of companies are increasing AI investments over the next three years, according to McKinsey's 2025 report. 

The budget is moving from headcount to infrastructure.

2️⃣ AI agents can be deployed in minutes using no-code platforms. 
You can scale on demand without hiring or training new team members.

3️⃣ Workers who adopt AI save an average of 7.5 hours per week, according to research from the London School of Economics. 

That time goes back into strategy and relationship building.

@Relevance AI positioned itself as the home of the AI workforce. 

Their no-code platform lets ops teams build and manage entire AI workforces without technical backgrounds.

Companies using @Relevance AI achieved 3,000% ROI by deploying AI agents across sales, support, and research functions, according to a MongoDB case study.

- Here is what an AI workforce looks like in practice:

→ Sales teamsdeploy AI agents that research prospects, draft personalized outreach, and book meetings 24/7. Human reps focus on closing deals.

→ Support teams deploy AI agents that triage tickets, draft responses based on knowledge bases, and escalate complex issues to humans. Response times drop, satisfaction rises.

→ RevOps teams deploy AI agents that process data, generate reports, and update CRMs automatically. Ops leaders shift from data entry to decision-making.

The shift from copilots to autonomous agents is happening now. 

By 2030, every team will have full AI workforces supporting them, not just AI assistants.

@Relevance AI raised $37.2M from investors including Insight Partners and Galileo Ventures to build the infrastructure for this shift.

Full breakdown below 👇

title: Intent Signals Claude Code
body:
Every LinkedIn post your team publishes is a goldmine of warm intent signals.

Most teams ignore it. We built an agent to mine it automatically.

Here's what we built with Claude Code:

When someone likes, comments on, or shares one of our LinkedIn posts, our agent:
• Scrapes all engagers via a scraping API
• Sends them to Clay for enrichment
• Runs ICP scoring + CRM de-dup + blocklist checks
• Finds their work emails
• Routes them into Instantly for an outbound sequence

The whole thing runs weekly on autopilot, deployed on Railway so it never stops.

Before Claude Code, I was paying $300+/month for dedicated scraping tools.

Now I pay nothing extra — and it's more tightly integrated with our stack.

We also extended it to monitor competitor posts and keyword-based posts. Anyone posting about "ABM" or "go-to-market"? We scrape their audience and push them into LinkedIn Ads as a custom targeting segment.

Are you using LinkedIn engagement as an outbound signal?

title: The 4-File Folder That Runs a Full Outbound Agent
body:
This is the exact folder structure of a Claude Code outbound agent.

4 files. A fully autonomous outbound machine.

Here's what each one does:

CLAUDE.md — The brain.
Every session starts here. It tells the agent what tools exist, what API credentials to use, and the rules it must follow. Think of it as a map — without it, the agent has no direction.

scoring-criteria.md — Your ICP rubric.
Company size, industry, funding stage, web traffic. The agent reads this and scores every company automatically — no manual qualifying, no guesswork.

copy-framework.md — Your playbook in plain English.
The copy that's already working for you, saved permanently. The agent reads this and writes every email sequence in your voice, without you touching it.

templates.md — Your output standard.
Examples of what good output looks like. The agent uses these as a benchmark so every sequence follows the same format.

The pipeline runs like this:

companies.csv → Score (criteria) → Find T1/T2 contacts (Apollo) → Enrich emails → Write sequences (copy-framework) → Deploy to Instantly

5 steps. Zero manual work between them.

Before this:
→ Scoring criteria in a spreadsheet no one updates
→ Copy frameworks scattered across Google Docs
→ Credentials copy-pasted every session
→ Nothing talking to each other

Now it all lives in one folder.
The agent reads every file before it runs.
Update one file — every future campaign reflects it.

This isn't a chatbot. It's infrastructure.

How many of those 5 pipeline steps are you still doing manually?

ONE-PAGER.

[ companies.csv ] → [ Score ] → [ Find Contacts ] → [ Enrich Emails ] → [ Write + Deploy ]

Below:
Node 1 — companies.csv:
• Input list
• Any source works

Node 2 — Score:
• scoring-criteria.md
• Company size, industry, funding, web traffic
• Output: T1 / T2 / T3

Node 3 — Find Contacts:
• Filter T1/T2 only
• Apollo search + enrich

Node 4 — Enrich Emails:
• Email enrichment provider
• Find verified work emails

Node 5 — Write + Deploy:
• Read copy-framework.md + templates.md
• Generate sequence
• Push campaign to Instantly

BELOW THE FLOW — swap row in pill tags:
Apollo → Clay | Enricher → any API | Instantly → Lemlist | Scoring → any ICP | Copy → any style

title: Intent Signals > Firmographics (Prospeo)
body:
We ran the same outbound campaign twice: same ICP, same message, same sequences.

First version: 10,000 contacts. 4.2% reply rate.

Second version: 3,200 contacts. 6.8% reply rate.

The only difference was timing.

↳ The outbound landscape shifted in 2025. Firmographics alone stopped working.

VP of Sales at Series A SaaS, 50-200 employees? That ICP is perfect.

But half of them just signed 2-year contracts.

Another 30% froze budgets after layoffs.

Some are leaving their roles in weeks.

Perfect ICP. Wrong timing.

So we started layering intent signals with @Prospeo V2.

Instead of just firmographics, we layered in intent signals that show when a company is actually ready to buy.

📊 A practical example:

Client sells pipeline management software to SaaS companies.

Old targeting:

→ B2B SaaS, 50-200 employees, Series A-B, US-based

→ VP of Sales or CRO title

Result: 10,000 contacts, 4.2% reply rate

New targeting (with @Prospeo V2 intent filters):

→ Same firmographics

→ Sales dept grew 30%+ in last 90 days

→ Raised funding in last 6 months

→ Using Salesforce or HubSpot

Result: 3,200 contacts, 6.8% reply rate.

↳ Here's what's more interesting than the numbers.

The quality of replies changed. 

Old approach got "not interested right now" or "check back in 6 months." 

New approach got "actually we were just talking about this last week" or "what's your calendar look like?"

People were already thinking about the problem when we showed up.

↳ How we build these searches:

1️⃣ Start with firmographics

Industry, company size, funding stage, location.

2️⃣ Add growth signals

→ Department headcount growth

→ Recent funding rounds

→ Active job postings

3️⃣ Add tech stack filters

→ Current tools they use

→ Integrations we support

→ Competitors we replace

4️⃣ Filter for contact quality

→ Verified email only

→ Verified mobile only

→ No incomplete data

Export to CSV. Enrich in @Clay. Push to @Instantly.

This is how to not burning through 50,000 contacts per quarter but targeting 15,000 with 2x better performance.

↳ The three intent signals that work best:

1️⃣ Department growth

Sales team grew 30%+ in 90 days = they're scaling = they need infrastructure.

2️⃣ Recent funding

Fresh budget = procurement unfrozen. Companies that just raised are more open than companies 18 months into efficiency mode.

3️⃣ Job postings

Hiring SDRs = thinking about outbound. Hiring sales ops = thinking about tools. Active hiring = they're prioritizing that function.

Layer all three together and you're prospecting when companies are ready to have the conversation.

↳ If you're building lists based only on firmographics, you're playing a volume game. Contact enough people and some will be ready.

Timing-based targeting flips that. Smaller lists, higher relevance, better results

• Sales dept grew 30%+ in 90 days
• Raised funding in last 6 months
• Active job postings in target dept

Node 3 — Tech Stack:
• Current tools they use
• Integrations we support
• Competitors we replace

Node 4 — Contact Quality:
• Verified email only
• Verified mobile only
• No incomplete data

BELOW THE FLOW — output row:
→ Export to CSV → Enrich in Clay → Push to Instantly

STAT CALLOUT (side or bottom): '3,200 contacts. 6.8% reply rate.' Large bold numbers in amber/orange.

FOOTER: 'Smaller lists. Higher relevance. Better results.' Dark italic.
LOGO: bottom-right corner.

title: New Post
body:
We cut SMB list-building time by 50%.

One data source replaces five scattered tools.

Teams were taking 4 hours per campaign building prospect lists.

They jumped between Apollo, ZoomInfo, manual Google searches, and two enrichment providers. 

Each tool covered different markets. None had complete SMB data.

The cost added up fast. They burned enrichment credits on basic firmographic data. More time verifying than prospecting.

We consolidated everything into @OpenMart inside @Clay.

**↳ The data coverage:**

200M+ SMBs across US, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Puerto Rico, Ireland, UK, France, and Spain.

Data points other providers miss:

→ Google ratings for each business
→ Number of locations per company
→ Verified contact information

No secondary enrichment needed.

@OpenMart includes firmographics that used to cost extra credits from Clay or OpenAI APIs.

**↳ Our workflow:**

@OpenMart pulls SMB data → @Clay enriches and scores → @Instantly launches campaigns

Kenny (our Head of GTM) runs this for 25,000-company scoping projects. 

The tier scoring happens automatically in @Clay based on employee count, sales team size, and location count.

No external scoring costs. No per-lookup charges.

**↳ The impact:**

→ 4 hours per campaign became 2 hours
→ Data accuracy improved (fewer bounces, better targeting)
→ Cost per lead dropped 30%

**When this approach works:**

You target SMBs across multiple regions. Single-country enterprise targeting needs different tools.

You already use @Clay for enrichment. @OpenMart works best as an upstream data source feeding into your existing workflow.

You want to consolidate subscriptions. One source beats maintaining separate tools for each market.

P.S. We still use other data sources for enterprise targeting. @OpenMart excels at SMB coverage. Different tools for different TAM segments.

What's your biggest challenge with SMB list-building?

title: The Folder Structure That Makes Claude Code Compound
body:
Most people use Claude Code like a smarter terminal.                                                                                                       
                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  The teams getting real ROI are using it like a fully briefed employee that picks up exactly where it left off — every single session.                                                                             
                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  Here's how the folder structure makes that happen:                                                                                                                                                                  
                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  Every automation lives in its own folder. That folder is the agent's brain.                                                                                                                                         
                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
  Inside every folder we build:                                                                                                                                                                                       

  CLAUDE.md — the most important file. Tells Claude exactly what this project does, what tools are available, what the rules are, and what it should never do. Every session starts here.

  /docs/ — how the workflow works. Step-by-step logic, design decisions, why we made specific choices. When something breaks 3 months later, this is where we look.

  /reference/ — everything static the agent needs. API credentials, IDs, field names, endpoint structures. Claude Code reads this instead of asking you for it every time.

  /reference/troubleshooting.md — every error we hit gets documented here with the fix. Claude reads it before every session. It never makes the same mistake twice.

  /skills/ — reusable instructions for recurring tasks. Instead of re-explaining a process, we write it once as a skill. Claude calls it by name.

  /workflows/ — the actual output. The finished automation, script, or workflow file — ready to import and deploy.

  The process every time:

  1. Open Claude Code inside the project folder
  2. Claude reads CLAUDE.md and has full context instantly
  3. You give one instruction — it knows the tools, the rules, the history
  4. When it hits a new error, you fix it once and add it to troubleshooting.md
  5. When a process repeats, you turn it into a skill
  6. Deploy and it runs on its own

  The folder compounds over time.

  Every session makes it smarter. Every error that gets documented never costs you time again. Every new skill reduces how much you have to explain next time.

  Tasks that used to take 20–30 minutes of manual work per instance now run automatically in the background — because all the context, logic, and credentials live in the folder permanently.

  This is not a chatbot you prompt from scratch every time.

  It's infrastructure you build once and maintain like any other system.

  What would you build if every manual process in your ops had its own folder like this?
designInstructions:
ONE-PAGER. Dark background (#0D1117). White text. LinkedIn blue (#0A66C2) accents.

HEADLINE (top, centered): 'The folder that makes Claude Code compound.' Bold, large.
SUBHEAD: 'This is the structure we use for every automation.' Grey, smaller.

HORIZONTAL LAYOUT — folder tree on the LEFT, descriptions on the RIGHT.

Folder tree (monospace font, dark card):
project-folder/
├── CLAUDE.md
├── /docs/
│   └── HOW-IT-WORKS.md
├── /reference/
│   ├── credentials.md
│   └── troubleshooting.md
├── /skills/
│   └── [skill-name].md
└── /workflows/
    └── [output-file]

Right side — one-line descriptions:
• CLAUDE.md → Agent brain. Rules, tools, context.
• /docs/ → Step-by-step logic + design decisions.
• /reference/ → Static data Claude reads every session.
• troubleshooting.md → Every error fixed once, never repeated.
• /skills/ → Reusable instructions. Write once, call by name.
• /workflows/ → The finished output. Ready to deploy.

FOOTER: 'Not a chatbot. Infrastructure.' White italic, centered.
LOGO: bottom-right corner.

title: Claude Code: 57-Second Campaign
body:
We built a complete cold email campaign in 57 seconds.
Company list to 154 leads loaded in Instantly. Zero errors. Zero manual work.

Here is the full system.

1️⃣ Score the companies
Drop your company list into Claude Code. It reads your ICP scoring criteria and tiers every company — T1, T2, T3, disqualified — using a Python script on the raw CSV data. No guessing. No hallucination.

2️⃣ Find the contacts
Tell it the persona you want. Claude hits the Apollo API, pulls the right people at T1 and T2 companies, and returns a breakdown by seniority and contacts per company.

3️⃣ Enrich the emails
144 of 159 contacts had emails from Apollo. Claude then hit LeadMagic and Lima Data for the remaining 15. Got 10 more. Done in seconds.

4️⃣ Write the copy
Claude connects to Instantly via API, pulls your best-performing campaigns by reply rate, extracts the frameworks, and generates copy for the new campaign. No starting from scratch.

5️⃣ Create and load the campaign
Claude creates the campaign in Instantly, injects the copy, uploads all leads, and configures send settings, scheduling, and delivery optimization. All of it.

Before: company list → score manually → find contacts → enrich → write copy → build campaign → upload leads.
After: company list → one prompt → campaign live in Instantly.

The tools did not change. Apollo, Instantly, LeadMagic.
The layer connecting them did.

What part of your outbound setup would you automate first? 👇
source: YouTube — Kenny Head of GTM at ColdIQ

title: GTM Brain: Connected Outbound
body:
Clay. Apollo. Instantly. 3 tools running your outbound.
None of them talking to each other.

Apollo has your contacts. Clay has your enrichment. Instantly has your sequences.
Same data. Siloed everywhere. Every campaign starts from scratch.

We fixed this with a single Claude Code setup.

Here is how it works:

1️⃣ One folder. All context.
Company lists, contact data, copy frameworks, campaign analytics — saved locally. Claude reads all of it before doing anything.

2️⃣ One instruction file. All behavior.
A CLAUDE.md file tells Claude: use this scoring criteria, find contacts via Apollo, write copy from these frameworks, push to Instantly. Four lines. Nothing more.

3️⃣ Every campaign makes the next one better.
Claude connects to your Instantly account, pulls best-performing campaigns by analytics, extracts the copy frameworks, and builds on them automatically. The system compounds.

What you end up with is a single brain that knows your ICP, your tools, your copy, and your results — all in one place.

Old way: siloed tools, manual handoffs, starting from scratch every campaign.
New way: one context layer, everything connected, outputs that improve over time.

Have you tried centralizing your GTM context into one place? 👇
source: YouTube — Kenny Head of GTM at ColdIQ

title: Bottoms-Up Outreach Strategy
body:
Most outbound teams go straight to the VP.
We start with the SDR team. Here is why reply rates are higher.

The logic: by the time you reach the decision-maker, the internal conversation has already started.

Here is the approach:

1. Target the SDR and AE team first
Send resources, value, webinar invites, lead magnets. Low-stakes outreach. High open rate. They are not the gatekeeper — they are the internal amplifier.

2. Move up to managers and team leads
They are talking to their teams. Your name is coming up in internal conversations. Now you have context to reference when you reach out.

3. Reach the VP or CRO last
Two things have happened by now. Their team has seen your name. And you can say: "I've already been working with a few people on your team."

4-5 contacts per company. No more.
Enough to create internal noise. Not enough to burn the account.

After 60 days with no reply, re-engage with different people at the same company. The account stays warm. The conversation keeps moving.

Most outbound skips straight to the signature.
Bottoms-up builds the internal case before you ever get there.

What is your current approach — top-down or bottoms-up? 👇
source: YouTube — Kenny Head of GTM at ColdIQ

title: Self-Improving Copy via Instantly
body:
Claude Code reads your best-performing campaigns and writes your next one.

Not from a template you wrote once and forgot about.
From your actual live campaign data — reply rates, open rates, sequences that converted.

Here is how it works.

Claude connects to your Instantly account via API. It pulls campaign analytics, ranks by performance, and extracts the copy from the top-performing sequences.

Those become your live frameworks.

When you launch a new campaign, Claude:
→ Finds the best-performing template for your campaign type
→ Injects your new context — persona, offer, goal, date
→ Generates copy that mirrors what already worked

The longer you run it, the better it gets.
Every campaign you launch adds to the data Claude is learning from.

Most teams write copy from scratch every time.
Or they reuse old templates they vaguely remember working.

This does neither. It finds what worked, learns the pattern, and builds on it automatically.

Before: dig through old campaigns manually or start from a blank doc.
After: Claude pulls your best performers and writes the next one in seconds.

Are you systematically learning from your past campaigns or starting fresh each time? 👇
source: YouTube — Kenny Head of GTM at ColdIQ

title: 4-Line CLAUDE.md Outbound System
body:
Our entire outbound operation runs from a 4-line instruction file.

Here is what it says:

→ Score companies using this ICP criteria document
→ Find contacts via Apollo API
→ Write copy using our best-performing frameworks
→ Create and push campaigns to Instantly

That is it. Four lines. No code. No technical setup.

From here, Claude handles everything else:
→ Reads the API docs for each tool
→ Connects via API keys you provide
→ Decides what endpoint to use at each step
→ Batches work at 100-200 records to maintain quality
→ Saves all outputs so every campaign builds on the last

The file is called CLAUDE.md.
It is the brain behind the whole system.

Most people think automating outbound at this level requires an engineering team, expensive tooling, or months of setup.

It requires four lines of plain English and an afternoon.

What would your 4-line instruction file say? 👇
source: YouTube — Kenny Head of GTM at ColdIQ

title: Record Your Workday — Claude Code
body:
1 habit that will change how you use Claude Code:
Record your workday. All of it.

Not a workflow diagram. Not a list of tools you use.
A real recording of what you actually do, hour by hour.

Then give it to Claude Code and ask: what should I automate?

Here is why this works:

1. You stop guessing what to automate
Most teams automate the obvious stuff first. Claude will find the high-leverage tasks — the manual work you have done so many times you stopped noticing it.

2. The output is specific to your business
Not a generic playbook. A system built around how your team actually operates, the tools you use, and the results you are already getting.

3. Claude already has your context
Your ICP, your copy, your tools, your process. Feed it once. It builds on it forever.

The scariest thing about Claude Code is the terminal. It looks like a developer tool.
Once you open it, you realize it is just a chat interface — the same as asking Claude or ChatGPT a question.

The only difference is that this one can actually do things.

Start there. Record a day. See what comes back.

What is one task in your GTM workflow you do manually every single day? 👇
source: YouTube — Kenny Head of GTM at ColdIQ
